briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence for Solo Karaoke at 6462 Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council considered a recommendation to support a liquor primary licence — a licence that allows a business to serve alcohol as its main activity — for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. Council voted unanimously to forward a recommendation to the provincial Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch (LCRB) in favour of issuing the licence, following a review of previous allegations against the business.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the LCRB's final decision — it will determine whether Solo Karaoke can legally serve alcohol.

briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence for Solo Karaoke on Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council voted unanimously to recommend that the provincial Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch (LCRB) — the body that issues liquor licences in BC — approve a liquor primary licence for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. A liquor primary licence allows a business to serve alcohol as its main activity. The recommendation follows a staff investigation into previous allegations about the business.
briefs.action_required: If you operate a nearby business or have concerns, contact the BC LCRB directly — Burnaby's recommendation now goes to them.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace Entire Zoning Bylaw, Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a completely rewritten Zoning Bylaw — the rulebook that controls what can be built, where, and how. The proposed bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, and would introduce new rules on building sizes, parking, landscaping (including required permeable surfaces that absorb rainwater), and minimum unit sizes for rental and ownership housing.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed Zoning Bylaw before April/May 2026 — it could affect your property or development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw introduced to Council — public review opportunity
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw adoption by Council
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Regulate Bear Spray Sales and Raise Parking Fees
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council advanced several proposed bylaws — including new rules that would require businesses selling bear spray to follow specific licensing conditions, and a fee increase for on-street public pay parking. Council also moved forward with proposed rezoning for several large mixed-use developments, and voted to suspend its $63,000 annual membership payment to the Federation of Canadian Municipalities until Burnaby gets a seat on the Big City Mayors' Caucus.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, watch for the new Burnaby Business Licence bylaw — it will add conditions to your licence.
briefs.industries: Retail, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider Liquor Licence Application for Solo Karaoke Ltd. in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation — meaning a formal presentation by the applicant — regarding a liquor licence application (LLA #25-12) for Solo Karaoke Ltd., located at 102-6462 Kingsway. Council will consider whether to support this application before it moves forward through the provincial licensing process. No decision has been made yet.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it could affect nearby businesses or set a precedent for similar licence applications.

key_dates.label: Solo Karaoke Ltd. liquor licence application — Burnaby Council delegation hearing

briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence approval for Solo Karaoke on Kingsway
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to recommend a Liquor Primary Licence — a permit allowing a business to serve alcohol as its main activity — for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. After a 9-week investigation by City staff, Burnaby RCMP, and the provincial liquor regulator found no evidence of illegal alcohol service, staff are recommending Council support the application.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — it could affect nearby businesses or set a local precedent.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Solo Karaoke liquor primary licence recommendation

briefs.title: Council to vote on sending concerns about proposed Metro 2050 regional growth changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on sending a formal letter to Metro Vancouver opposing four proposed changes to Metro 2050 — the regional plan that guides where and how growth happens across the Lower Mainland. The proposed changes, requested by the mayors of Surrey, Langley Township, and Delta, would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (the line that separates urban development from rural and agricultural land), potentially increasing costs for utilities, transit, and development charges.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — expanded urban boundaries could raise Development Cost Charges for your project.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment options comment period closes — Metro Vancouver
Metro Vancouver Board considers initiating Metro 2050 amendment process
Metro Vancouver Board considers final reading of Metro 2050 amendment bylaw

briefs.title: Council to vote on granting BC Hydro access to City land at 4111 Norland Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on giving BC Hydro a Statutory Right-of-Way — a legal permission to use a strip of City-owned land — at 4111 Norland Avenue. This would allow BC Hydro to install, operate, and maintain electrical infrastructure needed to power the new Community Safety Building being built on that site.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this proposal affects City-owned land and does not impact private businesses.

briefs.title: Council to vote on applying for up to $200,000 to speed up Burnaby development approvals
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to apply for a provincial grant of up to $200,000 through the UBCM Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved and the grant is awarded, the funding would help the City streamline (speed up and simplify) its building and development permit processes — including faster electrical hookups for small housing projects, clearer pathways for prefabricated and modular homes, and staff training to handle applications more consistently.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — faster permit approvals could affect your development or construction timelines.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — UBCM Development Approvals grant application authorization

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Renewing Burnaby's IT ticketing software licence with ServiceNow
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract to ServiceNow, Inc. worth roughly $580,985 (including taxes) to renew the software licences that power the City's internal IT help-desk system. The contract would run from March 16, 2026 to March 15, 2029, and would be purchased directly from the software maker to get better pricing than the City's previous arrangement.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on March 10, 2026 — this is an internal City IT contract with no direct impact on most businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby ServiceNow IT ticketing licence contract start date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Vote on Parking, Grants, and Recreation Plan
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several committee reports were up for approval — including changes to the Community Grant Policy, a draft Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan (which would move to a second round of public engagement), and a proposal to study new off-street parking spaces near Pender Street. The parking item was referred back to staff for further review, with a report due back to Council within four months.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the parking study report back to Council — it could affect parking access near your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby off-street parking feasibility study — staff report back to Council deadline

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Community Grant Policy and Application updates
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on changes to Burnaby's Community Grant Policy and application form. The proposed updates would clarify what grants can and cannot be used for, require non-profits to have a Burnaby address, narrow Food Security grants to ongoing programs (not one-time events), and keep the current equal-split funding method when grant requests exceed available funds.
briefs.action_required: If you run a non-profit in Burnaby, review the proposed policy changes before the March 10, 2026 council meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Grant Policy amendments — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's first new Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan since 1984
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a draft long-range plan called 'Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and Culture.' If approved, the plan would set service targets across 15 areas — including parks, arenas, aquatics, and cultural programs — and authorize a second round of public engagement this spring before a final version is brought back to Council for adoption.
briefs.action_required: Attend a public drop-in or complete the online survey this spring to share your input before the plan is finalized.

key_dates.label: Burnaby PRC Plan — Council approval vote at regular meeting
Burnaby PRC Plan — second round public engagement begins
Burnaby PRC Plan — engagement results reported to PRCC
Burnaby PRC Plan — final plan expected at Council for approval
Burnaby PRC Plan — implementation phase expected to begin

briefs.title: Council to vote on supporting Tourism Burnaby's bid to host 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on whether to support Tourism Burnaby's plan to submit a bid to host the 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference. This is an 'in principle' endorsement — meaning a show of support that lets Tourism Burnaby move forward with a proposal by May 2026 — and does not commit the City to any funding.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision if your business operates in tourism, hospitality, or event services.

key_dates.label: Tourism Burnaby 2028 CUFC bid submission deadline

briefs.title: Council to Consider New Public Parking Lots Near Burnaby Heights on Pender Street
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on referring a proposal back to staff that would use the Off-Street Parking Reserve Fund — money collected from developers who paid cash instead of building parking — to study and potentially build new public parking lots on two vacant city-owned properties at 4301/4305 and 4171 Pender Street, near the Hastings Street commercial strip. The Planning and Development Committee has already raised concerns about whether parking is the best use of these lots versus housing or public space, so the matter is being sent back to staff for further review before any final decision is made.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10 Council meeting — the outcome could affect parking availability near Burnaby Heights businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Pender Street parking proposal referred to staff

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council Consent Agenda Items — Public Safety, Finance & Recreation Updates
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several consent agenda items were set for approval, covering status updates on public safety (RCMP, Fire Department, and a youth task force), city finances (Gaming Reserve, Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve), and a new recreation facilities project plan. Council voted to receive these reports for information, meaning no immediate rule changes are coming — but the updates signal where city resources and priorities are headed.
briefs.action_required: Review the RCMP and Fire Department status updates if your business is near a Burnaby community safety area.

briefs.title: Council to receive update on $20.1M Trans Mountain safety fund for Burnaby Mountain
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve — a $20.1 million agreement with Trans Mountain Corporation that runs from 2024 to 2043. The funds are earmarked for emergency planning and safety projects, including a new fire station, a hazardous materials response vehicle, an alternate access route on Burnaby Mountain, and wildfire detection technology.
briefs.action_required: Monitor how these safety investments near Burnaby Mountain may affect your business operations or emergency planning.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Construction & Development, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to consider: Status update on Burnaby's 2025 Casino Gaming Reserve funds
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on Burnaby's Gaming Reserve — a special fund fed by 10% of net casino proceeds from Grand Villa (Gateway Casino). As of December 31, 2025, the City spent $12.7M from this reserve and held a balance of $11.7M. No new spending decisions are being made at this meeting; Council will simply be informed of the fund's current state.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Status Update on Seven Major Burnaby Civic Construction Projects
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on seven large city-owned building projects currently in planning, design, or construction. Projects include a new RCMP building, two community centres, a major aquatic and arena complex, a theatre renewal, and a restaurant expansion — all reported to be on schedule and on budget as of February 2026.
briefs.action_required: Monitor project timelines if your business is near construction sites — road and parking impacts are ongoing.

key_dates.label: Mintara Restaurant Expansion — project handover to City anticipated
Community Facilities Fit-Out Project — initial procurement processes planned
James Cowan Theatre Renewal — project handover to City anticipated

Cameron Community Centre and Library — project handover to City anticipated

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Youth Sexual Exploitation Task Force
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on receiving a status update from the Burnaby–New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth. The report covers the Task Force's recent activities — including information sharing, a professional development event on online exploitation, and plans for future workshops in 2026.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Update for December 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update report from the Burnaby Fire Department covering activity through the end of December 2025. The report is informational only — no new rules or requirements are being proposed — and Council will simply vote to acknowledge it.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby RCMP Quarterly Status and Performance Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on local RCMP policing operations for August–November 2025, along with a quarterly performance report covering July–September 2025. The report covers crime and traffic statistics, community policing initiatives, and online crime reporting — no new rules or costs are proposed, and no action is required from businesses at this time.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Information Reports — Burnaby Meeting March 10, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a section of information reports at its March 10, 2026 meeting. No body text or specific report details were provided, so the exact topics under consideration are not yet known. Business owners should monitor the full agenda for any items that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for March 10, 2026 to see if any reports affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — information reports reviewed

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Parking Meter, Fees, and Business Licence Bylaws Up for Approval
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council gave first, second, and third readings to three proposed bylaws — covering parking meter and EV charging rules, consolidated fees and charges, and bylaw notice enforcement. Council also gave final adoption to amendments affecting business licences, fees and charges, and zoning at several Burnaby addresses. These changes are moving through the approval process and some are now in force.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business licence, parking use, or property zoning is affected by the newly adopted Burnaby bylaws.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Business Licence Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026 — final adoption
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026 — final adoption
Burnaby Parking Meter and EV Charging Meter Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026 — third reading passed
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Amendment Bylaw No. 2, 2026 — third reading passed
Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Amendment Bylaw No. 2, 2026 — third reading passed

briefs.industries: Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on parking meter and EV charging fee bylaw changes
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council gave first, second, and third reading — meaning initial approval steps, but not yet final sign-off — to three proposed bylaws. These would update rules and fees for parking meters and electric vehicle (EV) charging meters, adjust the city's general fees and charges, and change how bylaw fines are enforced. A final adoption vote is still required before any of these changes take effect.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final adoption vote — new parking, EV charging, and bylaw fine rules could affect your business costs.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging meter rules, adding energy-based fees and time limits
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update the rules for public electric vehicle (EV) charging meters in the city. The proposed changes would allow charging fees to be based on energy used (kilowatt-hours) — not just time parked — and would let the City set maximum time limits for how long a driver can occupy a public EV charging space.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you use or operate public EV charging spaces, new fee structures and time limits may apply.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on new EV charging fee rates for public stations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the fees charged at Burnaby's public electric vehicle (EV) charging stations. The new rates would charge drivers either by energy used (per kWh) or by time, and would add 'idle fees' — extra charges for vehicles that stay plugged in after charging is done — to encourage faster turnover at public chargers.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business has public EV chargers in Burnaby — these new rates could affect your customers.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: New Fine for Overstaying Time Limits at Burnaby Public EV Charging Spots
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would add a new fine for drivers who exceed the maximum time limit at public electric vehicle (EV) charging meters in Burnaby. If approved, the penalty would be $75, with an early payment option of $60.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how long you or your customers park at EV charging stations.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby Zoning Bylaw text amendment — third reading and final adoption
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 meeting, Burnaby Council was set to give third reading and final adoption to Bylaw No. 14792, a text amendment to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965. A text amendment changes the written rules in the zoning bylaw — the rules that control how land and buildings can be used — without changing the zoning map itself. Council voted unanimously to adopt the bylaw at this meeting.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby zoning bylaw text amendment details to see if your property or business use is affected.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw text amendment (No. 14792) — final adoption date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Five new zoning districts under Burnaby's 2050 land use plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw that would create five new zoning districts — defined areas that set rules for how land can be used and what can be built there. This change is designed to put the Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (OCP) into effect, which is the city's long-term blueprint for how land across Burnaby will be used and developed.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10, 2026 council meeting — this vote could affect how your property or site can be used.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 2, 2026 — council vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw Amendment Up for Final Adoption
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several bylaws were given final adoption, including an amendment to the Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw 2017 (Bylaw No. 14798) and related changes to fees, charges, and bylaw enforcement. These changes are now adopted and may affect how business licences are issued, enforced, or priced in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Check with the City of Burnaby to confirm if your business licence fees or conditions have changed.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Business Licence Bylaw Amendment No. 1, 2026 — final adoption date
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw Amendment No. 1, 2026 — final adoption date
Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw Amendment No. 1, 2026 — final adoption date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 50-storey rental tower and community hub rezoning
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14744) that would rezone land at 7135 Walker Ave. and 7244 Arcola St. to allow a 50-storey rental apartment tower alongside a 6-storey community hub. The development would include roughly 480 rental units — about 96 at below-market rents — plus retail space, offices, an auditorium, and community services such as a food bank.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council adoption vote in 2026 — this rezoning could affect nearby property and retail tenants.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw No. 14744 (7135 Walker & 7244 Arcola) — Council adoption vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Council to vote on revised self-storage building design at Still Creek Ave, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the approved plans for a self-storage facility at 3944 and 3942 Still Creek Avenue. The changes would remove one underground parking level, take self-storage units out of the underground area, adjust how vehicles enter and exit, and raise the building's flood level — resulting in a six-storey building instead of the originally approved five-storey design.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council vote — if you operate nearby or have an interest in this site, track the final decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 14763 — final adoption vote (Council meeting)

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on adding 6 storeys of rental housing at 5889 Barker Avenue
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would allow six extra storeys of market-rate rental apartments to be built on top of an already-approved six-storey non-market rental building at 5889 Barker Avenue in Burnaby. If passed, the result would be a 12-storey rental building with a mix of rent levels — some units at below-market rates and some at market rates.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council adoption vote on March 10, 2026 — this affects rental housing supply near your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 19, 2025 — final adoption vote (5889 Barker Ave)

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Restrict Who Can Sell Bear Spray
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would limit bear spray sales in Burnaby to outdoor and sporting goods retailers only — no general retailers. If approved, these sellers would need to check photo ID, refuse sales to anyone under 19, lock up their stock, and keep detailed sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, check whether your business qualifies as an 'authorized vendor' under the proposed rules.

key_dates.label: Burnaby bear spray sales bylaw — council adoption vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Fines for Bear Spray Sales Rule Violations Up for Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would add $500 fines for businesses that break Burnaby's bear spray sales rules — such as selling to minors, skipping ID checks, or failing to keep proper sales records. There are no discounts on these fines and no compliance agreements (a deal to reduce the penalty by following the rules) available.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, review your ID, storage, and record-keeping practices before this bylaw passes.

key_dates.label: Burnaby bear spray sales fines bylaw — council adoption vote

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to cut plumbing permit fees for some developments before June 30
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for most developments (excluding small-scale multi-unit housing and tenant improvements) if the permit is issued on or before June 30, 2026. Under the proposal, the fee for the first fixture would drop from $121 to $70, and each additional fixture from $67 to $39. Owners who already paid the higher fee between January 1 and March 10, 2026 could receive a refund for the difference.
briefs.action_required: If you pulled a plumbing permit in Burnaby since Jan 1, 2026, watch for the council vote — you may be owed a refund.

key_dates.label: Burnaby reduced plumbing permit fee window closes — application deadline

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update how advisory body rules are created and changed
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would change how 'terms of reference' — the rules that govern how advisory bodies operate — are created and updated. Under the proposed change, the Mayor would direct the preparation of these rules, which would then go to the Executive Committee of Council for review before Council votes on them.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final adoption vote — this affects how Burnaby's advisory bodies are governed.

briefs.title: No Action Items: Burnaby Council Correspondence Package — March 10, 2026
briefs.summary: The March 10, 2026 Burnaby City Council agenda shows no correspondence items were submitted for Council's decision. All other correspondence received has been published in the Council Correspondence and Public Notice Submissions package for that date. There is nothing for Council to vote on under this agenda item.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Acting Mayor Schedule Change and Planning Meeting Reschedules
briefs.summary: At the March 10, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, councillors voted to update the Acting Mayor schedule for 2026 and reschedule several Planning and Development Committee meetings throughout the year. These are administrative housekeeping decisions — they affect when and how city planning meetings are held, which could matter if you are tracking a development application or permit.
briefs.action_required: Check the new Planning and Development Committee dates if you have an active permit or rezoning application in Burnaby.

briefs.title: Council to vote on Acting Mayor schedule changes for March and September 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a change to its 2026 Acting Mayor schedule. The proposal would appoint Councillor Lee as Acting Mayor for March 2026 and Councillor Gu as Acting Mayor for September 2026, replacing whoever was previously assigned to those months.

briefs.title: Council to consider rescheduling five Burnaby Planning and Development Committee meetings in 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on moving five Planning and Development Committee (PDC) meetings to new dates in 2026. If approved, meetings originally set for April 1, May 6, July 8, September 2, and October 7 would shift by one to two days earlier, with some also changing their start time to 10 am.
briefs.action_required: Check the new PDC meeting dates if you plan to attend or present at any 2026 planning hearings.

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to Set Rules on Tenant Eligibility Dates for Rezoning Sites
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on new criteria that would decide which tenants qualify for help under Burnaby's Tenant Assistance Policy (TAP) — a program that supports renters displaced by redevelopment — when a developer resubmits a rezoning application. Under the proposed rules, the original eligibility date (the date the first rezoning application was filed) could be kept if two conditions are met: tenants were informed early and given assistance forms, and any new tenants who moved in after that date signed a document confirming they are not eligible for TAP benefits.
briefs.action_required: If your rental property has an in-stream rezoning application, review whether your tenants have signed the required TAP addendum.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Tenant Assistance Policy eligibility criteria vote

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development
